coredata.dc:title: Adsorptive performance of alginate/carbon nanotube-carbon dot-magnesium fluorohydroxyapatite hydrogel for methylene blue-contaminated water
coredata.dc:creator: Mallakpour, Shadpour
Behranvand, Vajiheh
Mallakpour, Fereshteh
coredata.dc:description: Bioadsorbents are promising materials for pollutant removal due to their high sorption capacity, low toxicity, and low cost. The present work describes the hybridization of carboxyl functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (f-CNTs) with carbon dots (CDs) and magnesium fluorohydroxyapatite (MFA) nanoparticles. The prepared f-CNT-CD-MFA nanostructure hybrids were incorporated into the alginate matrix to prepare alginate/f-CNT-CD-MFA hydrogel composite beads. After full characterization, the obtained beads were used as an adsorbent of methylene blue (MB) from an aqueous solution. Removal yields of pure alginate and reinforced alginate were compared. Better fitness of experimental data was obtained by the linear form of Freundlich and nonlinear-PSO kinetic models for MB sorption by alginate/f-CNT-CD-MFA beads. In addition to the correlation of determination values, four error functions were employed to determine the best-fit isotherms and kinetics models. The phenomenological study showed that the rate-controlling step for the MB adsorption by the composite is film diffusion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium alginate
Magnesium fluorohydroxyapatite
Carbon dot
Carbon nanotubes
Dye removal

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The novel aspect of this work
Effect of pH and adsorbent amount

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Affairs Division of Isfahan University of Technology
Center of Excellence in Sensors and Green Chemistry (IUT), Isfahan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial assistance provided by the Research Affairs Division of Isfahan University of Technology (IUT), Isfahan. I. R. Iran, Iran National Science Foundation (INSF), Tehran, I. R. Iran (Grant Number 97010316 ) and Center of Excellence in Sensors and Green Chemistry (IUT), Isfahan, I. R. Iran. We would like to express our special thanks to Dr. H. Farrokhpour and Dr. F. Tabesh for their kind guidance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of (a) f-CNT, (b) f-CNT-CD [28], (c) f-CNT-CD-MFA, (d) pure CaAlg, (e) CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 2 wt%, (f) CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 6 wt%, and (g) CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 10 wt% (digital images of pure polymer and composites also have been shown beside their FE-SEM images).
TEM images of (a) f-CNT [28], (b) f-CNT-CD (c) f-CNT-CD-MFA, and (d) CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 10 wt%.
(A) TGA thermograms of f-CNT, f-CNT-CD [28], and f-CNT-CD-MFA, (B) TGA thermograms of pure CaAlg and composites.

(A) Effect of pH, (B) Effect of contact time, linear plots of (C) pseudo-first-order (PFO) kinetics, and (D) pseudo-second-order (PSO) kinetics, and (E) intraparticle diffusion, (F) non-linear models of PFO kinetics, and PSO kinetics of MB sorption onto the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% and (G) comparison of removal efficiencies of various samples (C0 = 10 mg L−1, pH = 10, T = 25 °C).

Adsorption isotherms of MB on CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% with linear models of (A) Langmuir and (B) Freundlich, and (C) non-linear models of Langmuir, Freundlich, and D-R, and (D) Possible mechanisms for interaction between MB and CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite.
The preparation procedure of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite beads.
Obtained Kinetic sorption parameters using various models (C0 = 10 mg L-1, pH = 10, T = 25 °C).
Error analysis values for the kinetics results of MB sorption onto CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PFO: pseudo-first-order kinetics, PSO: pseudo-second-order kinetics.
The lowest Chi-square, RMSE, SSE, and HYBRID are in bold.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Linear and non-linear equilibrium modeling parameters for the MB sorption onto the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The highest R2 are in bold.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Error analysis values for the equilibrium modeling of MB sorption onto CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The lowest Chi-square, RMSE, SSE, and HYBRID are in bold.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamic constants of the MB sorption at different temperatures.
The results of the reusability test for the MB sorption onto CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt%.

head.ce:title: Adsorptive performance of alginate/carbon nanotube-carbon dot-magnesium fluorohydroxyapatite hydrogel for methylene blue-contaminated water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 84156-83111, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Organic Polymer Chemistry Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 84156–83111, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomaterials Group, Department of Materials Engineering, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan 8415683111, Islamic Republic of Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biomaterials Group, Department of Materials Engineering, Isfahan University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biomaterials Group, Department of Materials Engineering, Isfahan University of Technology, Isfahan, 8415683111, Islamic Republic of Iran

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Current address: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Michigan Technological University, Houghton, Michigan 49931, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bioadsorbents are promising materials for pollutant removal due to their high sorption capacity, low toxicity, and low cost. The present work describes the hybridization of carboxyl functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes (f-CNTs) with carbon dots (CDs) and magnesium fluorohydroxyapatite (MFA) nanoparticles. The prepared f-CNT-CD-MFA nanostructure hybrids were incorporated into the alginate matrix to prepare alginate/f-CNT-CD-MFA hydrogel composite beads. After full characterization, the obtained beads were used as an adsorbent of methylene blue (MB) from an aqueous solution. Removal yields of pure alginate and reinforced alginate were compared. Better fitness of experimental data was obtained by the linear form of Freundlich and nonlinear-PSO kinetic models for MB sorption by alginate/f-CNT-CD-MFA beads. In addition to the correlation of determination values, four error functions were employed to determine the best-fit isotherms and kinetics models. The phenomenological study showed that the rate-controlling step for the MB adsorption by the composite is film diffusion.

ce:section.ce:para: A significant environmental problem is the pollution of freshwater with dyestuff. Numerous industries, for example, the leather, textile, paper, rubber, plastic, wood, and food industries widely used organic dyes. Most of the organic dyes are non-biodegradable, and highly toxic due to having aromatic amines, and even carcinogenic to human life [1–3]. Methylene blue (MB) as a cationic dye is applied in many fields such as silk or cotton dyeing, textile, and printing, which can cause harmful influences such as vomiting, irritation, and cancers [4,5]. Among approaches that are used for pollutant removal, adsorption as the most favorable method is widely employed for pollutant removal because of low cost, high effectiveness, and ease of manner. A key point in removing pollutants from water is using sorbents that do not generate additional pollution and have a low cost [6,7]. The biosorption method introduces biomolecules such as fungi, bacteria, seaweeds, and algae with high sorption capacity and low-cost. Alginate is an anionic polysaccharide extracted from marine brown algae and has the potential of sorption in the elimination of contaminants [7–10]. For improvement of the sorption capacity of alginate, recent research groups have used different materials. For example, nano-hydroxyapatite coated activated carbon impregnated alginate was used for uranium removal [11]. Alginate/waste peanut shell-based activated carbon for the elimination of Direct Blue-86 dye [12], alginate/Fe3O4 nanoparticles for Cu(II) uptake [13], magnetite graphene oxide encapsulated in alginate beads for the Cr(VI) and As(V) adsorption [14], alginate/sugarcane bagasse biochar for MB sanitization [15], and alginate/biomass of Saccharomyces cerevisiae for the adsorption of toxic metals of Pb(II), Cr(VI) and Cd(II) [16] have been designed and employed.

Carbon nanotubes (CNT)s have hollow and layered structures with high specific surface areas, which are ideal for pollutant sorption but need to be functionalized to prevent their aggregations, improve their dispersion in the organic matrix and decrease their toxicity [17,18].

Freundlich, Langmuir, and Dubinin–Radushkevich (DR) are equilibrium isotherm models that can determine the adsorption mechanism. Due to its simplicity and utility, many researchers have used linear regression analysis to determine the appropriate isotherm model [19–21]. In this method, the magnitude of correlation of determination (R2) determines the quality of fits of experimental data. Nevertheless, in recent years isotherms linearization has been criticized because by transforming and location of various axial for different linearized models, the error distribution alters. While in nonlinear method data, sets are not transformed, so it doesn’t have the disadvantages of the linear method. Because of the existence of some challenges about using linear or nonlinear models, it would be more proper to fit experimental data of sorption by both methods [22,23].

In this study, magnesium fluorohydroxyapatite (MFA) with superior biocompatibility, biological properties, and more chemical stability [24] and carbon dots (CDs) as an emerging class of carbon nanomaterials (with particle size below 10 nm) were employed for the surface modification of carboxyl functionalized multi-walled CNTs (f-CNTs) simultaneously, to prepare fCNT-CD. Recently, Chang et al. used hydroxyapatite supported N-doped CDs and studied the photocatalytic performance of the hybrid for degradation of MB [25]. Zhang et al. employed a layered double hydroxide–CD composite for the removal of methyl blue [26]. As can be found, CNTs, CDs, and hydroxyapatites have exhibited acceptable sorption and photocatalysis properties upon pollutants. So, it is expected that their combination can enhance and bring together their characteristics. In this study, alginate was applied as a polymer composite matrix. Crosslinked sodium alginate by calcium ions and preparation of calcium alginate (CaAlg) beads is an operative way for the pollutants withdrawal from wastewater because it is non-toxic, biodegradable, simple in design and operation, and have low cost [27]. Then, the ternary hybrid consists of fCNT-CD and FA was synthesized and incorporated into the alginate using ultrasonic waves as a fast method for the fabrication of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite beads to remove MB from the aqueous solution. Adsorption isotherm data for MB elimination were studied by both linearized and non-linearized approaches, and the results were compared.

The red pepper was provided from a supermarket and washed before usage. f-CNTs with characteristics cited in our previous work [28] were supplied by Neutrino Co. (Tehran, I. R. Iran). Sodium alginate (SA) (product number 18097, 15–25 cps, 1% in H2O, Mw of 120 × 103-190 × 103 g mol−1, and the ratio of mannuronate/guluronate = 1.56) and calcium chloride (CaCl2·2H2O) were provided from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany) and Riedel-de Haën (Seelze, Germany), respectively. MB, as well as other solvents, were provided by Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany).

For the characterization of the samples, FT-IR spectra [Jasco-680 (Japan)] were obtained in the region 4000–400 cm−1 in the KBr pellet. Phase analysis was done using a Philips X′PERT MPD (Germany) with a copper target at λ = 1.54 Å, a current of 35 mA and a voltage of 40 kV (2θ range of 10–80°). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) accompanied by mapping was accomplished by TE-SCAN MIRA III (Czech Republic). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were used for the morphology study using TE-SCAN MIRA III (Czech Republic) and HITACHI S-4160 (Japan) (acceleration voltage: 15 kV). Philips CM120 (Netherlands) was used for the obtaining of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. Optical microscopy images were provided utilizing a digital camera microscope [U-VISION MV500 (China)]. N
2 adsorption-desorption isotherm was obtained using BELSORP-mini II (Japan) device to get the specific surface area (SBET), mean pore diameter, and pore volume values. ZAG CHEMIE PTR 79 Co. (Tehran, Iran) with a glass electrode was used for the pH adjustment.
CDs were prepared from red pepper according to the reported procedure of Yin and coworkers [29]. The fCNT-CD was obtained through our reported procedure [28].
Hybrid of f-CNT-CD-MFA was obtained according to our previous study [30]. Scheme 1 shows the process steps and stable and well-dispersed suspension of f-CNT-CD-MFA after sonication.

SA solution (2% w/v) was prepared by 1 h stirring of SA in DI water and its sonication for 10 min (Topsonics ultrasonic liquid processors, Tehran, Iran, the power of 100 W and frequency of 20 × 103 Hz). Then, different percentages of f-CNT-CD-MFA (2, 6, and 10 wt% related to pure alginate) were added to the alginate solution, and the mixture was stirred for 2 h at RT, then was sonicated for 1 h (the power of 100 W) in an ice bath. It should be mentioned that the dispersion stability was examined every 15 min to optimize the sonication time. Finally, the best stability was obtained after 1 h. Afterward, the suspension was slowly extruded drop-wise by a syringe (20 drops per minute) into a CaCl2 aqueous solution (0.1 mol L−1) under constant stirring for crosslinking. The beads were formed rapidly and stirred for 24 h for better crosslinking. After this time, washing of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite beads was done with 300 mL DI water for the removal of additional amounts of CaCl2 that remained on the surface of the bead to attain neutral pH. Then, drying was done in an oven at 60 °C overnight. For the comparison purpose in sorption properties, pure CaAlg without doping as well as reinforced CaAlg with MFA NPs (10 wt% related to polymer) according to the above process were synthesized as well. Scheme 1 shows the procedure for the fabrication of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite beads along with the interactions between the alginate matrix and filler.

The MB sorption from aqueous solutions onto the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% was examined in a batch system. In each step, 10 mL of MB solution with known concentration was added into a predetermined amount of adsorbent in a flask and was shaken at 298 K. The pH effect was studied by changing pH values from 4 to 10. Adsorbent amounts were changed from 20 to 100 mg to examine removing MB dye [initial concentration (C0) = 10 mg L−1, V = 10 mL] from aqueous solution. The contact time influence was tested by contacting 100 mg of the adsorbent with 100 mL MB of definite concentration (10 mg L−1 of MB). Lastly, the influence of C0 of MB (5–100 mg L−1) on the adsorption process of MB was studied. Using UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Rayleigh UV-1800, Beijing, China), the equilibrium concentrations of MB in the sorption solutions were obtained by applying calibration curves for MB at a maximum wavelength of 662 nm. To study the influence of temperature, 20 mg of the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite sorbent was equilibrated with the MB solution (10 mg L−1, 10 mL) at temperatures of 298, 318, and 328 K.

Calculation of the adsorption capacity at a time (qt, mg g−1) and equilibrium (qe, mg g−1) was accomplished using the Eqs. (1) and (2) [15,31]:
(1)
q
t
=
C
0
−
C

m
× V
(2)
q
e

C
0
−
C
e
m
× V
where Ct and Ce denote t time and equilibrium concentrations of MB (mg L−1). m demonstrates the mass of adsorbent (g), and V describes the volume of solution (L).

For reusability inspection of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt%, first, adsorption experiments were accomplished by shaking of 20 mg sorbent into 10 mL of MB solution (at two different concentrations of 60 and 80 mg L−1) at pH 10 for 48 h. The adsorbents were separated easily by decanting the upper solution, washed with 40 mL distilled water; then, 25 mL HCl solution (0.1 mol L−1) was added to it. After shaking for 3 h, the beads washed many times with distilled water, dried at 60 °C, and was reused for the next sorption cycles. These experiments were repeated for three cycles.

Deciding about the fit goodness based on R2 solely is not suitable. Consequently, in addition to R2, four different error functions such as Chi-square Test, Sum of the Squares of the Errors (SSE), Hybrid Fractional Error Function (HYBRID), and Residual Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) were also considered [22,23]. The related equations are as follow:
(3)

In all equations, y is the calculated sorption values from isotherms, and x is the experimental sorption values. The number of experimental points is shown by “n”, and “p” denotes the number of parameters of the equation. The drawing of graphs was done via Igor Pro software, version 6.12, and calculations were accomplished by Microsoft Office Excel 2019.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The novel aspect of this work
ce:section.ce:para: As it was reviewed, hybrids of hydroxyapatite supported N-doped CDs [25], and layered double hydroxide–CD [26] have been used for the removal of pollutants. Also, there are reports about the preparation of scaffolds of hydroxyapatite and alginate composite, which have the potential of use in tissue engineering [27,32,33]. In this work, MFA, which have superior biocompatibility, biological properties, and more chemical stability than FA as well as CDs were employed for the surface functionalization of f-CNTs to not only increase functional groups and provide more accessible sites for better adsorption of pollutants by alginate but also increase the bioactivity of the system and decrease toxicity [34]. It is important to use safe materials in the design of adsorbents because they can enter the water environment and cause secondary pollution. To make a long story short, CDs and CNTs contain adsorption active sites such as carboxyl groups, hydroxyl groups, and a conjugated structure, which can form electrostatic, H-bonding, and π-π interactions with dye pollutant and MFA provides more bioactivity in the system. To our knowledge, no work is available on the study of the effect of ternary hybrid of f-CNT-CD-MFA on the physicochemical and adsorption properties of alginate.

The FT-IR spectra of fCNT, pure CD, fCNT-CD, pure FA, f-CNT-CD-MFA, pure CaAlg, and CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% were given in Fig. S1. In the f-CNT-CD spectrum, besides the peaks related to the pure CD, the stretching vibration of the conjugated C˭C of f-CNT at 1578 cm−1 as well as a new peak at around 1721 cm−1 (formation of ester carbonyl) are detected. In the FT-IR spectrum of f-CNT-CD-MFA, the new absorption band at around 1043 cm−1 and broadness of peaks below 700 cm−1 can be related to different vibration bands of the PO4 3- in FA. Characteristics bands of pure CaAlg can be observed at 1644 cm−1 and 1434 cm−1 related to asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the –COO-. In the spectrum of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt%, this band appears to be shifted from 1644 to 1610 cm−1, which is possibly related to the H-bonding interaction between the oxygen sites of alginate with the f-CNT-CD-MFA. Also, the stretching vibrations of O‒H in CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% seemed narrower than pure CaAlg, which can be due to the changes in the interaction of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of alginate with functional groups in f-CNT-CD-MFA structure. It was shown by the green circle.

Fig. S2 displays XRD patterns of pure MFA [24], f-CNT-CD [28], f-CNT-CD-MFA, pure CaAlg [35], and CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composites of 2, 6 and 10 wt%. Pure MFA exhibited sharp and narrow crystalline peaks. The main diffraction reflections of CNT were observed at 2θ = 26° and 44°. The XRD pattern of f-CNT-CD-MFA demonstrated the reflections of both MFA and f-CNT-CD, which were marked by asterisks and circles. No crystal peak was seen for pure CaAlg, which represents the amorphous construction of it. Due to the good dispersion of f-CNT-CD-MFA in the alginate matrix, no clear crystalline peaks can be detected in the patterns of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composites.

Fig. 1 shows the microsurface constructions of the f-CNT, f-CNT-CD, f-CNT-CD-MFA, and CaA/f-CNT-CD-MFA composites. Packed and aggregated nanotubes can be observed in FE-SEM images of f-CNTs, while after the functionalization of tubes by CDs, dispersion of them was obvious. Good attachment and uniform distribution of FA particles on the CNTs’ surface can be observed from Fig. 1c. Digital photographs of the wet CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite beads demonstrated a black color with a quasi-spherical shape owing to the presence of f-CNT-CD-MFA, representing the good incorporation of f-CNT-CD-MFA in the alginate matrix. Optical microscopy images of the beads showed that different percentages of f-CNT-CD-MFA as filler did not have any influence on the shape of the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA bead composites. Compared to the smooth surface of pure alginate, CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composites had a porous and wrinkled surface (Fig. 1e–g), which can be useful for toxic pollutants removal from an aqueous solution.

EDX mapping analysis was carried out for the chemical characterization and the spatial distribution of particular elements of the samples. Fig. S3 presents the EDX spectra of f-CNT-CD-MFA nanohybrid and shows the existence of major elements such as strong C and O peaks, with Ca and Mg. The uniform distribution of f-CNT-CD-MFA throughout the alginate matrix was confirmed by obtained EDX mapping results of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 10 wt% and was shown in Fig. S4. TEM images of f-CNTs, f-CNT-CD, f-CNT-CD-MFA, and CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% are shown in Fig. 2. As can be seen after CNT functionalization by CDs (Fig. 2b) and MFA (Fig. 2c), aggregation of nanotubes decreased significantly compared to the f-CNTs (Fig. 2a). The presence of MFA as circular shapes is observed in Fig. 2c. Fig. 2d shows the dispersion state of nanotubes in the alginate matrix.

Fig. S5 shows the N
2 adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K for CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt%. The obtained SBET for CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% was 2.7 m2 g−1. However, SBET increased around 3 times compared to the pure alginate (about 0.17 m2 g−1) [36], but its surface is low yet, so it can be said that numerous functional groups will have a key role for sorption by this composite. Due to the IUPAC classification, it exhibited a type III isotherm with an H3 hysteresis loop, indicating its mesoporous structure. Also, the mean pore diameter was obtained 8.6 nm, which is in the range of mesoporous materials (2–50 nm). The molecular size of the MB is only 1.4 nm [37], so the mesoporous CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% (with a mean pore diameter of 8.6 nm) can efficiently capture molecules of MB.

Thermograms of f-CNT, f-CNT-CD, and f-CNT-CD-MFA are shown in Fig. 3A. A small mass loss below 200 °C for the samples can be related to the adsorbed water molecules. As can be observed, both f-CNT, and f-CNT-CD showed high thermal stability, and more than 80 wt% of their masses were preserved till 800 °C. While for hybridized f-CNT-CD by MFA, a sharp mass loss was started at about 250 °C due to the decomposition of MFA and CD. Thermograms of the pure CaAlg and composites are shown in Fig. 3B. Onset decomposition temperatures (Td10%: the temperature at which 90% mass remained) increased around 12–14 °C compared to the pure CaAlg, which shows the higher thermal resistance of the composites than the pure polymer. Besides, when the f-CNT-CD-MFA content increased to 10 wt%, both T10% and char residue at 800 °C enhanced compared to the pure and other composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and adsorbent amount
ce:section.ce:para: An important variable that should be optimized in the adsorption process is pH. As can be seen from Fig. 4A, with increasing the pH, the removal percentages slowly increased, and variation of pH had only a slight effect on sorption properties of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt%. The value of pKa of the carboxyl group is around 4.2, so the carboxyl groups on the surfaces of f-CNTs and CDs are deprotonated in pHs more than 4.2. In pHs above 4, no significant changes were observed in the adsorption capacity of MB. It shows that enough deprotonated ‒COO- groups were reached for electrostatic interaction between composite beads and cationic dye [38]. This phenomenon was observed by Benhouria et al. [37], Guesmi et al. [39], and Song et al. [40]. According to the results, pH 10 was selected as the optimum pH for further steps. The influence of the amount of adsorbent on the percentage of MB elimination efficiency was examined (Fig. S6). The outcomes presented that by rising the adsorbent amount from 20 to 100 mg, the adsorption of MB increased from 60% to 90% and then reached 94% when the amount of adsorbent increased to 120 mg due to increasing of accessible active sites for adsorption. Also, the adsorption capacities (mg g−1) were calculated to be 3.14, 1.77, 1.32, 1.07, 0.90, and 0.78 for the amounts of 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 mg, respectively, which this negligible decrease can be due to the creation of aggregates that decrease the accessibility of the efficient sorption area. Also, it can be said that at a low adsorbent amount, active sites are fully occupied by MB, leading to surface saturation and yielding higher adsorption capacity [41,42]. Because there was no considerable difference between the removal efficiencies in the adsorbent amount of 100 and 120 mg, 100 mg was selected as the optimal amount of adsorbent.

Fig. 4B gives the contact time effect on the adsorption capacity of MB by CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt%. When the contact time increased, the qt increased, and the equilibrium was obtained after 48 h. There are enough active sites at the beginning of the adsorption, so qt increases sharply. After that by increasing the time the sorption sites are filled and the number of free sites reduces, which leads to a plateau in the diagram and the equilibrium time reaches. Adsorption kinetics were examined by following linear and non-linear equations (Eqs. (8)–(11)).
(8)
Linear
form
of
PFO

]
(10)

k
2
q
e
t
Where qe and qt are the dye adsorption capacity of adsorbent (mg g−1) at equilibrium and at a certain time t (min), respectively. Rate constants of PFO and PSO are shown by k1 (min−1) and k2 (g mg−1 min−1), respectively. Fig. 4C, D, and F present the kinetics results, and the extracted results from both models are listed in Table 1. To this Table, the higher value of regression coefficients (R2) was attained from the non-linear form of the PSO model. Besides, the theoretical value of qe (qe,calc) provided by this model was close to the experimental qe (qe,exp). Based on values obtained from error functions ( Table 2), the experimental results were well fitted by the non-linear form of the PSO model as well, which exhibits the control of adsorption rate by the chemical adsorption [43].

To examine the adsorption mechanism of the MB adsorption on CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% and study the adsorption rate-limiting step, the intraparticle diffusion model was employed, and the Weber-Morris graph of qt versus t1/2 was taken (Eq. (12)). Where ki (mg g−1 min−0.5) is the rate constant of intraparticle diffusion, and Ci provides an idea about the boundary layer’s thickness [44].
(12)
Intraparticle
diffusion
q
t
=
k
i
t
1 / 2
+
C
i

The presence of two linear sections displayed two diffusion stages which show several joint adsorption mechanisms; the first was the MB diffusion from the solution to the external surface of the adsorbent and then passing the boundary layer (film diffusion), and the second stage was owing to the intraparticle diffusion (pore diffusion) (Fig. 4E). The pore and film diffusion coefficients were calculated by Eqs. (13) and (14) to confirm which one is the rate-controlling step:
(13)
Pore
diffusion
coefficient
D
p
=
0.03
r
0
2
t
1 / 2
(14)
Film
diffusion
coefficient
D
f
=
0.23
q
t
δ
r
0
C
0
t
1 / 2

The terms q
t , C0, r0, and δ, denote the amount of MB adsorbed, initial concentration MB, the radius of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% (10−1 cm), and film thickness (10 −3 cm) [45], respectively. If the value of D p is in the range of 10−11–10 −13 cm2 s −1, pore diffusion controls diffusion, while for the film diffusion-controlled adsorption process, the D f value can be between 10−6 and 10−8 cm2 s −1. In this work, D p and D f values were obtained 2.04 × 10−4 and 5.48 × 10−7, respectively, which shows pore diffusion is not the rate-controlling step and the MB removal by the composite is controlled by the film diffusion. Also, the rate of external surface adsorption was higher than the second one because of more accessed active sites and low competition between the dye molecules in this stage [32]. Fig. 4G presents a comparison of the removal efficiencies of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% (76%) with pure CaAlg (40%), CaAlg/MFA 10 wt% (42%), CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 2 (51%), and 6 wt% (68%). As can be observed pure CaAlg and CaAlg/MFA composite 10 wt% showed the lowest removal efficiencies. After using carbon nanostructures (f-CNT-CD) and incorporating into the alginate matrix, the removal efficiency was improved, considerably. Also, it should be mentioned that significant improvements in removal efficiencies were obtained by increasing the percentages of f-CNT-CD-MFA from 2 to 10 wt%.

Isotherm models of Freundlich, Langmuir, and Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) were employed to provide key information on the mechanism of adsorption, the surface features, and affinities of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite as an adsorbent. All resulted isotherms from linear and non-linear models are shown in Fig. 5. Isotherm models of linear and non-linear are presented below:
(15)
Linear
form
of
Langmuir
C
e
q
e
=
C
e
q
m
+
1
k
L
q
m
(16)
Non − linear form of Langmuir
q
e
=
K
L
q
m
C
e
1 +
K
L
C
e
(17)
Linear
form
of
Freundlich
lnq
e
=
1 n
lnC
e
+
lnK
F
(18)
Non − linear
form
of
Freundlich
q
e
=
K
F
C
e
1 / n
(19)
Linear form of D − R ln
q
e
= ln
q
m
−
K
DR
ε
2
(20)
Non − linear form of D − R
q
e
=
q
m
exp ⁡ ( −
K
DR
ε
2
)
Where Ce (mg L−1), qe (mg g−1), and qm (mg g−1) are the equilibrium adsorbate concentrations, equilibrium sorption capacity, and maximum sorption capacity of adsorbent, respectively. KL (L g−1), KF, and n are the constants of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms. D-R isotherm constant is KDR (mol2 kJ2). Obtained parameters from linear and non-linear models are given in Table 3, and error values for both types of different models are listed in Table 4. The lowest value of error shows that experimental results are close to the obtained ones from models. Therefore, the model with the highest R2 and lowest error values is appropriate to describe the adsorption process. In this work, a comparison between R2 and error function values showed that the Freundlich model in linear form has the highest R2 and lowest error functions, so it shows the best fit between experimental and calculated data. This model states nonhomogeneous and multilayer adsorption. The 1/n value extracted from Freundlich isotherm was obtained between 0 and 1 and presents favorable adsorption of MB on CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite [44,46,47].

In our opinion, because the Freundlich linear model and not Langmuir one provided a better explanation about the adsorption of MB on CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite, comparison of qmax of this work with other adsorbents is not reasonable. Overall, comparison of the obtained adsorbent with other reported ones from a structural and economical point of view showed that in this work a polymer composite beads based on alginate matrix as a low-cost biopolymer were prepared and reinforced by a very low amount of f-CNT, CD, and MFA to obtain a low cost and efficient adsorbent, simultaneously. In contrast, the number of expensive species in other reported adsorbents was higher than the cost of alginate. Also, the prepared adsorbent has another advantage. CNTs have been functionalized by biomaterials, so their accession into the environment may not have a toxic effect on mother earth. Also, these nanostructures have been stabilized in the alginate matrix and cannot leach easily. Each of the constituents of filler is containing abundant functional groups, which can improve adsorption.

The influence of solution temperature on MB remediation was investigated and the linear plot of ln k
d versus 1/T was drawn. The values of standard enthalpy changes (ΔH°), standard entropy changes (ΔS°), and standard Gibbs free energy (ΔG°) were calculated from Van’t Hoff Eq. (21), and Eq. (22) are listed in Table 5. The ΔH° values were negative, demonstrating the exothermic process of sorption, which clarifies the decrease of removal efficiency with increasing temperatures from 298 to 328 K.
(21)
ln
K
d
= −
∆
H
0
RT
+
∆ S
0
R
(22)
∆
G
0
= − RT ln
K
d
Where T is the absolute temperature (K), R is the universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and the distribution adsorption coefficient Kd is considered by Kd = (qe/Ce) × 1000. The spontaneous process of sorption was confirmed by negative values of ΔG° at various temperatures. It has been reported that ΔG° values in the range of − 20 to 0 kJ mol−1 and ΔH° values between 8 and 25 kJ mol−1 shows physical adsorption [6,48]. In this study, according to the obtained amounts of ΔG° and ΔH°, earlier claims can be confirmed, and it can be said that the main adsorption mechanism of the procedure is physical kinds such as electrostatic interaction. The very low amount of ΔS° value may be due to the MB fixation to the exchanger sites causing a reduction in the degree of freedom of the system [9].

A noteworthy economic aspect of the water treatment procedure is the regeneration of adsorbent. If a dye has a weak interaction with sorbent can be simply desorbed by water at neutral pH. However, dye attachment by electrostatic attraction or ion exchange can be desorbed under highly alkaline or acidic (like HCl) conditions [49]. In our system, desorption was not happened by water and ethanol, and the adsorbed MB was removed using HCl (0.1 mol L−1), which proved the electrostatic nature of MB interaction to the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite. On the other hand, as seen in Table 6, the qe and R% values of the adsorbent increased considerably after the first cycle. It is because after contacting beads by HCl, the protonation process can happen, and the H-form of biocomposite beads replaced Ca-form is created. H-form sorbent has a higher MB binding affinity compared to the Ca-form [50]. Finally, the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite sorbent can exhibit high reusability performance even after the 3rd cycle. It should be mentioned that composite beads can be efficiently and simply separated and recovered by decanting.

The EDX spectra and quantitative results obtained for CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite 10 wt% before and after MB adsorption are compared in Fig. S7 and Table S1, respectively. The presence of elements such as N, S, and Na is related to the MB structure, which are found in the structure of composite after adsorption. Different kinds of interactions can be proposed for MB sorption onto the CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composite, but it seems two mechanisms are more probable. The electrostatic interactions can be formed between the negative COO- groups of the alginate and filler and the positively charged MB. Hydrogen bonding is another interaction between the –COOH and –OH on the surfaces of alginate, f-CNT, CD, and MFA with MB (Fig. 5D). The spectra of FT-IR of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA 10 wt% after MB adsorption (Fig. S8) can confirm these results. Broadening the peak at 3419 cm−1 (due to –OH groups) shows new H-bonding interactions between dye and adsorbent. Shifts in the peaks from 1616 cm−1 to 1601 cm−1 as well as 1419 cm−1 to 1412 cm−1 (owing to C˭O stretching of the carboxylic acid of alginate) as well as the increase in peak intensity in the mentioned area can be related to the involvement of these groups in the MB adsorption by electrostatic interactions. Besides, because MB contains the C˭C bonds and CD and CNTs have a conjugated structure, π-π interactions can be created amongst them [51,52].

To summarize, CDs and MFA were used for the functionalization of f-CNTs and the preparation of a novel f-CNT-CD-MFA hybrid. The hybrid was incorporated into the alginate matrix to improve the physicochemical and adsorption properties of the alginate. Sonication waves as a fast and green way were used for the preparation steps, which provided good interfacial adhesion between the filler and matrix and lead to an improvement in the thermal resistance of pure alginate. No crystalline peaks were observed in the XRD patterns of CaAlg/f-CNT-CD-MFA composites owing to the nice dispersion of f-CNT-CD-MFA in the polymer matrix. Mesoporous structure of composite was confirmed by BET analysis. Due to the presence of carbonaceous materials such as f-CNTs and CDs, the adsorption ability of alginate increased. Herein two important and updated subjects in adsorption processes that are phenomenological studies and linear and non-linear forms of isothermal and kinetics models for MB adsorption were examined in detail. Different error functions such as Chi-square Test, SSE, HYBRID, and RMSE in addition to R2 were used to make statistical data more reliable. A comparison between linear and non-linear forms of adsorption isotherms, as well as kinetic models for MB adsorption, showed better fitness of data by the linear form of Freundlich and nonlinear-PSO kinetic models. This bio-adsorbent contains green and safe materials, which means that discharging into the environment may not hurt the mother earth. The obtained composites were easily reused from the water for several cycles, and this can be appreciated from an economic point of view.

Shadpour Mallakpour: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Vajiheh Behranvand: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

We would like to express our special thanks to Dr. H. Farrokhpour and Dr. F. Tabesh for their kind guidance.
